target_hypotheses.7: David Tso is the warmest unconverted contact in the entire pipeline — he replied to the pinned tweet organically before any DM was sent. He runs Base ecosystem and ventures, giving him a direct line to 5-10 x402 builders simultaneously. This is the last day of the loop; a single reply from David is worth more than any other first-contact reply because the success metric is an intro, not a walkthrough. The ask is a one-question DM about where authorization fits in the Base x402 stack — lowest-friction conversation with highest potential leverage.
target_hypotheses.2: Nathan Schwermann won the Cronos x402 Hackathon ($24K) specifically for building permission-scoping in a payment chain — the tightest ICP match in the entire pipeline for the Bolyra problem. He didn't just participate in an x402 hackathon; he built the exact delegation/scope layer that Bolyra extends with ZKP privacy. Three weeks post-win, he's actively shipping and thinking about the same architectural questions. The technical question in the draft is calibrated to the specific design decision he had to make — self-contained credential vs live registry call — which makes the question immediately recognizable and answerable.
target_hypotheses.3: Rodrigo Coelho built ampersend, described explicitly as 'the management layer for x402 payments.' Management layer means orchestration of agent payment flows means the authorization signal naturally lives there. If ampersend incorporates any authorization data, that signal propagates to every x402 flow running through it. He's also at Edge & Node, working on the graph protocol layer — he thinks in composable infrastructure and will understand the ZKP approach. Today is the last day to take the shot on a prospect who could be the integration that produces the loop's 'proof point' target.
target_hypotheses.21: Joao Moura is the CrewAI founder. The bolyra-crewai v0.2.0 integration (99 tests) is already built and PR #6382 is open on the CrewAI repo — this is the only prospect in the pipeline where there's a concrete code artifact waiting for a decision. The DM does not pitch; it opens with the CrewAI multi-agent trust boundary as a technical observation, leading to a question about how authorization currently works for agents making external paid calls within a crew. If he replies, the follow-up references PR #6382 immediately. A reply here is the fastest path to a measurable proof point before loop close.
target_hypotheses.23: Liam Horne co-created the Machine Payments Protocol with Stripe — the most credible architecture for AI agent payments. MPP specifies the payment envelope and deliberately leaves agent identity verification out of scope. That gap is the precise Bolyra value proposition. Liam made that architectural decision intentionally; the question in the draft asks whether it was a scope boundary or an open question — either answer is informative. He thinks in specs and protocols; a conversation about where identity fits in MPP is technically interesting to him independent of any product outcome.
target_hypotheses.9: Merit Systems built x402scan, Coinbase-endorsed as the canonical explorer for x402 transaction data. They index every transaction, which means they see the authorization gap more clearly than any other operator in the ecosystem — they know exactly what's in the x402 payload and what's missing. If authorization signals were present in x402 transactions, x402scan would be the natural place to surface them, making the authorization layer visible at network level. This is the infrastructure integration that creates the most durable proof point: 'Bolyra authorization signals visible in x402scan.'
target_hypotheses.28: Eric Ciarla is Firecrawl's co-founder. He publicly framed his x402 strategy as 'agentic commerce at internet scale' — the exact scale at which authorization failures become systematic rather than edge cases. PR #3902 from Bolyra is already open on the Firecrawl repo. Reaching Eric at the co-founder level (vs. Caleb, contacted day 1) creates a second thread at a different decision height. A reply from Eric is more likely to advance to a walkthrough than a reply from Caleb, because a co-founder conversation about scaling infrastructure is his native context.
target_hypotheses.36: Brett Calhoun replied 'congrats' before any outreach — warm inbound from a $25M VC focused on developer infrastructure and Web3. He is not an ICP design partner but his value today is portfolio introductions: Redbud portfolio companies building live x402 endpoints are the ICP. A single reply with a portfolio intro is worth more than 8 cold first-contacts that return nothing. The day-7 last-push timing is appropriate for the investor pipeline: loop ends today, last chance to convert the warm signal into a warm intro before the week closes.

drafts.7: Hey David, the framing you put out on x402 as the payment layer for the open agent economy is the right starting point — and the authorization question surfaces immediately when any wallet-equipped agent can transact without proving it was scoped to do so. For the x402 builders you work with on Base today, are you seeing anyone tackle the agent authorization problem beyond the wallet signature — or is that still a roll-your-own problem that each endpoint team handles independently?
drafts.2: Hey Nathan, winning the Cronos x402 Hackathon with a permission-scoping implementation is the most technically specific proof of the delegation problem I've seen come out of any x402 contest — you didn't just build on x402, you built the layer x402 is missing. For the scoped permission model you shipped: when an agent presents its credential to an x402 endpoint, is verification self-contained in the credential itself, or does the endpoint need to call back to the Cronos registry at payment time to confirm the scope is still valid?
drafts.3: Hey Rodrigo, ampersend building the management and observation layer for x402 payments is the right infrastructure position — whoever controls the orchestration layer will see the authorization signal question arrive as soon as operators start delegating payment initiation to agents. For the payment flows running through ampersend today, do you currently surface any caller identity beyond wallet address — something like which operator or agent was authorized to initiate a specific payment — or is that attribution not yet visible at the management layer?
drafts.21: Hey João, CrewAI orchestrating crews where sub-agents call external paid services is exactly the trust boundary where authorization needs to be explicit — a sub-agent that can initiate an x402 payment on behalf of a crew can do so without its operator ever scoping what it's permitted to spend on. For crews making x402 payments today, are builders implementing per-agent authorization checks within a crew, or does the orchestrator-level trust implicitly extend to all agents in the crew by default?
drafts.23: Hey Liam, co-creating the Machine Payments Protocol with Stripe as a transport-agnostic payment envelope for AI agents is the right infrastructure call — abstracting the payment layer so services don't need custom integrations per framework removes a key reason not to adopt. For the identity boundary in MPP: when a payee receives an agent payment through the protocol, is there a specified mechanism for verifying that the paying agent had explicit authorization from an operator to initiate that specific request, or was that intentionally left outside MPP's scope?
drafts.9: Hey, x402scan building the Etherscan-equivalent explorer for x402 transaction data is critical infrastructure — whoever holds the canonical record of x402 activity is positioned to make authorization signals visible at the network level when they exist. For the transaction data you index today, is there any caller metadata beyond wallet address in the x402 payload that you're already capturing, or would surfacing 'was this agent authorized by an operator?' require new data to be present at the origination point before it could appear in the explorer?
drafts.28: Hey Eric, 'agentic commerce at internet scale' is the right framing — and at that scale, an unauthorized agent hitting your x402 endpoint is indistinguishable from an authorized one unless there's an explicit identity signal beyond the wallet. For Firecrawl's x402 search endpoint, how are you thinking about caller identity today: is a valid payment the complete gate, or are you seeing operators ask for a way to scope which of their agents can call Firecrawl on their behalf?
drafts.36: Hey Brett, the congrats reply landed well — Redbud's thesis on developer infrastructure and Web3 puts you in the middle of the agent commerce ecosystem as it's forming. For your portfolio, are any of your investments building live x402 endpoints or paid agent flows where the question of 'which agent is authorized to pay?' is unresolved? That's the exact conversation I'm trying to have with builders this week — a warm intro to anyone hitting that problem would be the most useful thing.

codex_review: Two-pass review plus critical loop-state flags. Pass 1 — compliance: all 8 targets have status not_contacted in pipeline.json ✓. IDs 7, 2, 3, 21, 23, 9, 28 are first-contact; ID 36 is first-contact (warm, investor). Zero drafts mention Bolyra ✓. Zero drafts say 'I built' ✓. Zero drafts contain any link ✓. All 8 leads open with something specific to the target's work, framing, or product ✓. All 8 end with exactly one technical question ✓. Pass 2 — quality: all drafts are 2–3 sentences; questions are distinct and non-generic; ID 7 and ID 36 questions are calibrated to their non-ICP roles (ecosystem intro and investor portfolio, respectively) ✓. CRITICAL FLAGS FOR FOUNDER: (1) LOOP ENDS TODAY — day-7 kill criteria require ≥3 positive replies and ≥1 walkthrough booked from 40 contacts total. Current state: 7 contacts sent (day 1 only), 0 positive replies, 0 walkthroughs. The scorecard CANNOT meet the day-7 target unless DMs from days 2–6 are sent TODAY in addition to today's 8. There are 33 drafted, unsent DMs sitting in day-2.json through day-6.json. Founder action required: flush the highest-priority backlog before the day closes. (2) SEND PRIORITY ORDER — if time allows only a subset today, send in this order: (a) ID 21 (Joao Moura, CrewAI — PR #6382 is leverage), (b) ID 2 (Nathan Schwermann — closest ICP match, fresh hackathon winner), (c) ID 7 (David Tso — warm, highest multiplier on success), (d) ID 3 (Rodrigo Coelho — ampersend, proof-point candidate), (e) ID 23 (Liam Horne, MPP), (f) ID 28 (Eric Ciarla, PR #3902 leverage), (g) ID 9 (Merit Systems, x402scan), (h) ID 36 (Brett Calhoun, portfolio intros). (3) ID 21 FOLLOW-UP PROTOCOL — if Joao replies to the DM, reference PR #6382 in the next message immediately. Do not wait; the PR is the concrete artifact that earns the technical conversation. (4) ID 7 SUCCESS METRIC — David Tso is not ICP. A reply here is a success only if it produces an intro to a Base builder with a live x402 endpoint. Score his reply at 2 only if he names someone. (5) ID 36 SUCCESS METRIC — Brett Calhoun is investor pipeline. If he names a portfolio company building x402, that name is the next prospect. Score his reply at 2 only if it produces a specific intro or company name. (6) KILL-CRITERIA EVALUATION — after today, the founder must apply the day-7 decision matrix: if ≥3 positive replies and ≥1 walkthrough → close the pilot; if ≥3 positive replies but 0 walkthroughs → the offer (20-min test) is not converting, change the CTA; if <3 positive replies from ≥40 contacts → kill the current message, audit which prospects actually received DMs (pipeline.json sent arrays vs. target lists), and decide on a wedge change; if total sent < 40 → the loop did not execute as designed, and kill criteria cannot be fairly applied until the full pipeline is sent. (7) BACKLOG NOTE — the unsent drafts in days 2–5 remain valid and individually strong; the day-6 drafts (IDs 31, 12, 30, 34, 37) are also ready to send. The most valuable ones from the backlog beyond today's 8: day-4 ID 22 (Alfonso/Crossmint, already named the authorization gap publicly), day-4 ID 17 (xpay/awesome-x402, lowest-friction proof point), day-5 ID 29 (Shahzad Safri, ecosystem analyst who names the missing category), day-2 ID 11 (Jia Yaoqi/AltLayer, x402-as-a-Service with $14.4M).
